title: Harnessing Plasmonic Heating For Switching In Antiferromagnets
authors: H. Y. Yuan, Yizheng Wu, Olena Gomonay
abstract: Heat waste is a bottleneck in the development of green information technologies and much effort has been devoted to suppress the heating effect in both electronic and spintronic devices. Here we take an alternative approach and show that controllable heating at the nanoscale can actually benefit information processing. In particular, we study a hybrid nanostructure consisting of a metallic square frame and an antiferromagnetic (AFM) thin film and show that the plasmonic heating can reversibly switch two perpendicularly-oriented AFM domains without the assistance of magnetic fields and electric currents. The required switching energy is at the order 1 nJ, three to six orders of magnitude lower than the current-driven AFM switching. The physical mechanism arises from the thermal-induced strain fields inside the frame, which couple to and manipulate the magnetic orientation via magnetoelastic effect. The strain field direction can be well controlled by selectively exciting the longitudinal and transverse plasmon modes by varying the polarization of the waves, which readily allows for a reversible switching of the AFM vector. Our findings provide tremendous opportunities for optically manipulating the magnetism with ultralow energy consumption and may further promote the interdisciplinary study of photonics, acoustics and spintronics.
summary: This paper proposes a paradigm shift in spintronics by using heat as a functional resource rather than a waste product. By using plasmonic heating in a gold nanostructure, the researchers show that light polarization can control thermal strain to switch antiferromagnetic domains. This method achieves switching energies significantly lower than traditional electrical methods. The approach offers a highly efficient, scalable path for all-optical magnetic information processing.
main_problem: The challenge of high energy consumption and heat waste in traditional spintronic devices, specifically the difficulty of efficiently and reversibly switching antiferromagnetic (AFM) domains.
main_result: Demonstrated that controlled nanoscale plasmonic heating can reversibly switch AFM domains with energy consumption 3 to 6 orders of magnitude lower than current-driven methods by using polarization-dependent thermal strain.
method: Multi-physics numerical simulations using COMSOL Multiphysics (Maxwell's equations, heat diffusion, and thermal-elastic equations) combined with custom micromagnetic simulations.
model_or_system: A hybrid nanostructure consisting of a metallic (gold) square frame/nanowire integrated with an antiferromagnetic (NiO) thin film.
key_observables: Temperature distribution, strain field components (u_xx, u_yy), absorption cross-section, and the orientation of the Néel vector.
important_parameters: Switching energy ~1 nJ; NiO Néel temperature ~525 K; 1 ns laser pulse duration; 1.2 um wavelength; thermal diffusion length ~100 nm.
main_assumptions: The mechanism is robust at operating temperatures where temperature-dependent changes in NiO's thermal expansion and density are minimal (~1%).
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the hybrid structure and switching concept. Fig 2: Energy landscape and lattice deformation illustration. Fig 3: Wavelength dependence of temperature/absorption and strain anisotropy. Fig 4: Temporal evolution of strain and temperature. Fig 5: Radial dependence of temperature, displacement, and strain.
paper_structure: Introduction of the heat-as-a-resource paradigm, description of the hybrid Au/NiO system, detailed multi-physics simulation workflow, analysis of polarization-dependent strain generation, demonstration of reversible switching, and discussion of scalability and robustness.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of a gold nanoframe and mag- netic thin film hybrid structure. The antiferromagnetic vector within the frame can be reversibly switched by alternating the polarizations of the incident light, enabled by plasmon- induced selective heating and strain effects.

FIG. 2. (a) Energy landscape of the antiferromagnet with and without the strain field ume. The insets with arrows sketch the direction of the N´eel vector. (b) Illustration of the lattice deformation and the corresponding strain field in an elastic medium under a horizontal and vertical temperature gradient, respectively.

FIG. 3. (a-b) Temperature distribution and the correspond- ing strain profile in a hybrid structure of gold nanowire|NiO thin film. The nanowire is cuboid-shaped with dimensions 0.1×0.1×1.0 µm3 and the surface of the NiO film is 2×2 µm2. A single rectangular laser pulse (p-wave) with wavelength 1.2 µm, duration 1 ns, and peak power 0.4 W incident perpendic- ularly on the hybrid structure. The temperature and strain profile were recorded at 1 ns. All the thermal and optical parameters of Au and NiO, as well as the simulation method, can be found in the End Matter. Geometric parameters are the same as those in Fig. 3. (b) Snapshot of temperature profile and the corresponding strain distribution at the magnetic surface at t = 1 ns.
